# confluence-release-summary

A GitHub Action that writes a plain-English summary of your project to an
**existing** Confluence page, generated by Gemini from the repo's own docs.

For the people who won't read your README — product managers, support,
leadership. It refreshes itself when the product actually changes, and stays
quiet when it doesn't.

- **Major and minor releases only.** A patch is a fix; it doesn't change what the
  product does, so republishing would churn the page history and notify watchers
  for nothing.
- **No dependencies, no build step.** `node:` builtins and global `fetch`. It runs
  the same in a Python, Go or Java repo as in a Node one.
- **Language-agnostic.** Version gating reads git *tags*, not a manifest file.

## Usage

Which pattern you want depends on **who creates your releases**.

### A. A person cuts releases

GitHub UI, or `gh release create` run with a personal token. A standalone
workflow on the release event:

```yaml
name: Confluence summary
on:
  release:
    types: [published]

jobs:
  summary:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0        # REQUIRED — tags drive the version gate
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 22
      - uses: snyk-workshops/confluence-release-summary@v1
        with:
          gemini-api-key: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_API_KEY }}
          confluence-base-url: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_BASE_URL }}
          confluence-email: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_EMAIL }}
          confluence-api-token: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_API_TOKEN }}
          confluence-page-id: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_PAGE_ID }}
          version: ${{ github.event.release.tag_name }}
          release-notes: ${{ github.event.release.body }}
```

### B. Your CI cuts releases

**`release: published` will never fire.** Events raised by the default
`GITHUB_TOKEN` do not start new workflow runs, so if one of your workflows creates
the release, a `release:` trigger looks correct and silently never runs.

Chain the job inside the workflow that cuts the release instead — no event
involved:

```yaml
# in the same workflow as your `release` job
  confluence:
    needs: [release]                        # run after the release is created
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'     # that workflow also runs on PRs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v5
        with:
          node-version: 22
      - uses: snyk-workshops/confluence-release-summary@v1
        with:
          gemini-api-key: ${{ secrets.GEMINI_API_KEY }}
          confluence-base-url: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_BASE_URL }}
          confluence-email: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_EMAIL }}
          confluence-api-token: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_API_TOKEN }}
          confluence-page-id: ${{ secrets.CONFLUENCE_PAGE_ID }}
```

No `version` or `release-notes` here — there is no release payload to read them
from, so the action resolves the version from git tags.

The other option is a PAT for creating the release, so the event is attributed to
a user and pattern A works. That costs you a stored token to manage.

### Don't mix the two

`needs:` and `if:` belong to pattern B only. Copying them into pattern A breaks
it:

- `needs: [release]` names a job in the *same* workflow. Pattern A has one job, so
  GitHub rejects the workflow with "depends on unknown job".
- `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` is never true on a release event —
  `github.ref` is the **tag** ref (`refs/tags/v1.2.3`). The job silently never
  runs, which is the harder failure to spot.

## Configuration

| Secret | What it is |
|---|---|
| `GEMINI_API_KEY` | [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/apikey) key |
| `CONFLUENCE_BASE_URL` | `https://yourorg.atlassian.net/wiki` — **the `/wiki` matters** |
| `CONFLUENCE_EMAIL` | the Atlassian account the token belongs to |
| `CONFLUENCE_API_TOKEN` | id.atlassian.com → Security → API tokens |
| `CONFLUENCE_PAGE_ID` | the page to update |

> Without `/wiki`, requests land on **Jira**, which answers with its own HTML 404
> page. The action detects that and tells you, but it's the most common setup
> mistake by a distance.

**Finding the page id:** open the page → *•••* → *Page information*; the id is the
number in the resulting URL.

The page must **already exist**. This action only ever updates — a wrong id fails
loudly rather than scattering stray pages around a space.

### Inputs

| Input | Default | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `gemini-model` | `gemini-3.6-flash` | Bump when Google retires one |
| `version` | the triggering tag | |
| `release-notes` | — | Fed to the model alongside the docs |
| `extra-docs` | — | Extra files beyond `README.md`, comma/newline separated |
| `force` | `false` | Publish even when it isn't a major/minor bump |
| `dry-run` | `false` | Generate and print, publish nothing |

Output: `published` — `"true"` when a page was written.

With **no** `CONFLUENCE_*` values set the action skips with a notice, so a fork
still gets a green pipeline. With **some** set it fails, because a half-configured
integration would otherwise look like a successful no-op forever.

## Version gating

The release tag is compared against the **previous git tag**, so this works in any
language. `v` prefixes are fine (`v1.2.3` and `1.2.3` both parse, and can be mixed
in one repo) and ordering is numeric (`2.10.0` > `2.9.0`).

Only full three-part versions count. A **moving major tag** like `v1` is ignored
rather than treated as a release, so it can't be mistaken for the previous version
when working out whether a bump was major, minor or patch.

`fetch-depth: 0` is required. Without tags the action **fails** rather than
assuming a first release — assuming would republish on every patch, which is the
exact thing the gate prevents.

A `dry-run` always generates regardless of the gate (it publishes nothing, and
skipping would print nothing, which defeats the point). `force` overrides the gate
for a deliberate manual run; `dry-run` beats `force`, so a preview can never
publish by accident.

## What ends up on the page

Generated from `README.md` (plus any `extra-docs`) and the release notes. The
prompt asks for **what the project does and who it's for**, and to leave out
install steps, env vars, file paths, code, frameworks and CI.

Four things are enforced in code rather than trusted to the prompt, because the
output lands on a company wiki:

- **A tag allowlist.** Filtered to a safe subset of storage-format HTML.
  `<script>`, `<style>` and `<iframe>` are removed *with their contents*; only
  `http(s)` `href` survives. Disallowed tags are unwrapped rather than deleted, so
  no section silently disappears.
- **A truncation check.** `finishReason: MAX_TOKENS` fails the run. Length alone
  doesn't catch this — a page cut off mid-sentence is still long. On Gemini 3
  models `maxOutputTokens` is a **combined** budget for reasoning and output, so
  reasoning can consume most of it.
- **A minimum length.** A refusal or a near-empty answer fails rather than
  replacing a good page with a blank one.
- **Reasoning is stripped.** Thinking models can return their scratchpad as extra
  response parts; those never reach the page.

The page opens with a banner naming the version and warning that manual edits are
overwritten — worth leaving in place, because the next minor release will do
exactly that.

### Accuracy

An LLM describing a repo can state things that aren't true. The prompt forbids
inventing anything absent from the supplied text and runs at `temperature: 0`, but
**read the page after the first real run**, and point it at a scratch page first.

It can't protect you from a claim that's stale in your own docs — if your README
says "six modules" and there are now eight, the page will faithfully say six. Fix
the source.

The README is untrusted input: each document is wrapped in explicit delimiters and
the model is told to treat repository content as data, never instructions, so a
README can't easily steer the summary.

## Which Gemini API this uses

`POST /v1beta/models/{model}:generateContent`.

Google now recommends the newer **Interactions API** for new work. `generateContent`
is not deprecated and is stable, while Interactions is still on a `/v1beta/` path
and changed schema in May 2026 (`outputs` → `steps`). Worth revisiting when it
reaches `v1`, or if a model you want is only served there. The change is confined
to `callGemini()`.

## Dogfooding

This repo runs the action on itself — see
`.github/workflows/confluence-summary.yml`. It uses `uses: ./` rather than a
published tag, so a run exercises whatever is on the branch: if a change breaks
the action, it breaks here first.

Unlike a repo whose CI cuts its releases, `release: published` **does** fire here,
because releases are created by a person — only `GITHUB_TOKEN`-raised events are
suppressed.

To try it, set the five secrets on this repo (pointing at a scratch Confluence
page) and run:

```bash
gh workflow run confluence-summary.yml -f dry-run=true
gh run download --name confluence-summary
```

## Development

```bash
node --test summarize.test.mjs        # 55 tests, no network

# Generate and print without publishing:
VERSION=1.2.0 GEMINI_API_KEY=... DRY_RUN=true node summarize.mjs
```
